skill.name: hooks-automation
skill.description: Automates Claude Code development workflows with Claude Flow hooks for task setup, file validation, formatting, memory coordination, session tracking, and Git quality gates.
skill.summary: Coordinate Claude Code hook workflows for automated validation, formatting, session memory, and multi-agent development.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static external-command findings are largely true positives because the skill documents automatic Claude Code hooks that execute shell commands using interpolated tool parameters. I found no prompt injection text or confirmed malicious exfiltration, but the unsafe hook patterns create command-injection, sensitive-data logging, and filesystem risks that should block publication until mitigated.

high_findings.title: Unsafe Command Interpolation in Automatic Hooks
high_findings.description: The recommended hook configuration executes command hooks with values from tool parameters such as file paths, Bash commands, task text, grep patterns, and task results. These values are inserted into shell command strings, which creates command-injection risk when untrusted content contains quotes, shell metacharacters, or command substitution.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines show executable hook commands built directly from Claude Code tool parameters. This is a clear unsafe pattern even though the file is documentation and not executable code itself.

high_findings.title: Shell Hook Uses File Path Expansion in Test Command
high_findings.description: The automatic testing example builds a shell command from a tool file path and uses shell parameter expansion before running npm test. A crafted path could alter command behavior because the value is placed inside a command string executed by a hook.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The finding is based on a concrete hook command that combines a tool-controlled file path with shell syntax. Legitimate testing is possible, but the example lacks escaping or argument isolation.

medium_findings.title: Command Output and Results May Be Stored in Memory
medium_findings.description: The post-bash hook documents command-output logging and result storage in memory by default. This can capture secrets printed by commands, tokens in errors, or sensitive file paths and persist them beyond the original command execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly describes command logging and result storage. The risk depends on how Claude Flow stores memory and whether sensitive outputs are filtered.

medium_findings.title: Custom Hook Script Execution Requires Review
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches users to register JavaScript hook scripts that execute during tool use. This is a legitimate extension mechanism, but marketplace users need review controls because such scripts can read content, block operations, and store data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script execution behavior is documented directly. The example appears benign, so the risk is about capability and deployment context rather than confirmed malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File References Are Mostly Protective Examples
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged environment file names. The visible examples reference production.env and .env.production in the context of backups or protected-file responses, so I did not find evidence of secret reading or exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain sensitive filenames, but the surrounding text frames them as validation or manual-review cases. This supports a false-positive verdict for exfiltration while still documenting sensitive-file handling.

low_findings.title: Weak-Cryptography and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-crypto and reconnaissance alerts match ordinary documentation terms such as hook descriptions, session IDs, hashes, status checks, and metrics examples. I found no evidence of cryptographic implementation, credential harvesting, or network scanning in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a markdown guide and the sampled alert lines are benign descriptions or Git metadata commands. Some line-level analyzer matches are opaque, so confidence is high but not absolute.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automatic External Command Hooks
dangerous_patterns.description: Multiple examples register hooks with type command. These hooks execute npx, npm, test, git, jq, bc, cat, and shell scripts automatically during Claude Code operations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly visible across several executable shell examples. The danger comes from automatic execution, not from confirmed malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Command Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The pre-bash documentation includes an rm -rf example. It targets /tmp/cache and is presented as a safety-check example, but it still demonstrates destructive shell behavior that requires confirmation controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact command is visible and destructive, but the surrounding section describes safety validation and confirmation. This makes the risk moderate rather than malicious.

content.value_statement: Complex Claude Code projects need consistent checks, memory, and coordination across sessions. This skill shows how to configure Claude Flow hooks for validation, formatting, task tracking, and multi-agent workflow automation.
actual_capabilities: Explains pre-task, pre-edit, pre-bash, and pre-search hook workflows for Claude Flow.
Shows post-edit, post-bash, post-task, and post-search hooks for formatting, logging, metrics, and memory updates.
Documents session start, restore, and end hooks for context persistence and summary generation.
Provides Git hook examples for pre-commit, post-commit, and pre-push quality gates.
Describes MCP memory coordination patterns for agent status, progress, and completion updates.
Includes custom JavaScript hook guidance for project-specific validation logic.
limitations: Requires Claude Flow CLI and Claude Code hook support before the workflows can run.
Examples are documentation only and must be adapted for each repository.
Shell hook examples need escaping, allowlists, and review before production use.
MCP coordination features depend on configured MCP servers and memory storage policies.

use_cases.title: Standardize AI Coding Checks
use_cases.description: Define pre-edit and post-edit hooks that validate files, format code, and record workflow decisions across a team.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Multi-Agent Sessions
use_cases.description: Use task, memory, and session hooks to preserve context while several agents work on related implementation tasks.

use_cases.title: Add Git Quality Gates
use_cases.description: Adapt the Git hook examples to run tests, collect metrics, and stop low-quality commits or pushes.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up Basic Hooks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use hooks-automation to help me configure basic Claude Code hooks for edits, Bash commands, and session summaries in this repository.

prompt_templates.title: Design Safe Hook Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Use hooks-automation to design hook rules for my project. Include validation, formatting, command review, and protections for sensitive files.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Multi-Agent Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Use hooks-automation to plan a multi-agent workflow with pre-task setup, memory coordination, post-task reporting, and session restore behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Hook Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use hooks-automation to review my Claude Code hook configuration for command-injection risk, secret leakage, timeout issues, and missing quality gates.

output_examples.input: Create a safe starter hook plan for a TypeScript service.
output_examples.output: A concise plan with pre-edit validation, post-edit formatting, test selection, session summaries, and required manual review for sensitive paths.

output_examples.input: Review my hook workflow before enabling it for the team.
output: Suggests timeouts, logging limits, and secret redaction.

output_examples.input: Plan hooks for a multi-agent refactor.
output_examples.output: A staged workflow that starts a session, loads memory, assigns agents, records edit decisions, runs tests, and exports a final summary.

best_practices: Use allowlists and argument arrays for hook commands instead of interpolated shell strings.
Redact secrets from command output before storing logs or memory records.
Test hooks in a disposable repository before enabling automatic execution in active projects.
anti_patterns: Do not pass raw tool parameters directly into shell commands.
Do not enable cleanup or formatting hooks without backups and clear path limits.
Do not store full command output when it may contain secrets or customer data.

faq.question: Does this skill install hooks automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents Claude Flow hook commands and configuration patterns that users apply in their own environment.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code, with examples centered on Claude Code and Claude Flow.

faq.question: Is it safe to use as written?
faq.answer: Use caution. Several examples interpolate tool parameters into shell commands and need hardening before team or production use.

faq.question: Can it coordinate MCP agents?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes memory updates, agent spawning, status tracking, and session persistence through Claude Flow MCP-style coordination.

faq.question: Can it help with Git workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for pre-commit checks, post-commit notifications, and pre-push quality gates.

faq.question: What should I review first?
faq.answer: Review every command hook, sensitive file rule, memory storage setting, timeout, and cleanup action before enabling automation.
